evidence: config.RelationConfig imports query.RelationType; config.GrammarConfigLoader imports query.RelationType — two files in the config package import from the query package
query.QueryExecutor imports config.RelationConfig and utils.RelationPatternUtils — the query package depends on the config package
The above creates a circular package dependency: config → query and query → config

utils.RelationPatternUtils imports config.RelationConfig; utils.RelationUtils imports config.GrammarConfig, config.RelationConfig, and query.RelationType — the 'utils' layer depends on both config and query, inverting the expected layering for a utility package
model package has zero project-internal imports — clean data layer
indexer package has zero project-internal imports — cleanly isolated
exploration imports from config, model, query, utils but NOT from api — correct downward dependency
api is the only package importing from exploration — correctly at the top of the hierarchy

strengths: model package is a clean data layer with no project-internal dependencies — pure DTOs/records that any layer can safely import
indexer package is fully self-contained with no project package imports, making it independently testable and deployable
viz package is isolated with no project-level imports — pure computation layer
exploration correctly sits above query/config without importing from api — the one-way dependency is respected in the service layer
api is the unambiguous top of the hierarchy and does not leak downward; no lower layer imports from api
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: A single root-cause misplacement — RelationType defined in query rather than config — cascades into a real circular dependency between config and query, and also drags the utils package into depending on two different domain layers, undermining what should be a bottom-tier utility namespace.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase has a genuine circular package dependency: config↔query. Both config.RelationConfig/GrammarConfigLoader import query.RelationType, while query.QueryExecutor imports config.RelationConfig. This is a real structural defect that could cause issues with module systems or cause refactoring headaches. The root cause is clear (RelationType belongs in config, not query) and the fix is well-scoped, but it currently requires touching multiple files across two packages. The utils package issue (RelationPatternUtils and RelationUtils depending on config and query) is a second, milder violation: utils is not a general-purpose bottom layer but a domain-specific helper that reaches into the business layer. The rest of the codebase shows disciplined layering — model, indexer, and viz are cleanly isolated; api is correctly at the top; exploration respects the hierarchy. The circular dependency pulls the score down significantly from an otherwise clean architecture.

issues.summary: Circular package dependency between config and query via RelationType
evidence: RelationType is a grammar classification (SURFACE vs DEP) — semantically it belongs in the config or model package, not in query
issues.suggestion: Move RelationType from query to config (or model). Update all importers (GrammarConfigLoader, RelationConfig, RelationUtils, SketchHandlers, etc.) to use the new location. This eliminates the cycle while keeping RelationType's semantics co-located with grammar configuration.

issues.summary: utils package depends on config and query, inverting its expected role as a bottom-tier layer
evidence: RelationPatternUtils imports config.RelationConfig — operates on domain objects, not generic utilities
RelationUtils imports config.GrammarConfig, config.RelationConfig, and query.RelationType — three imports from two domain packages
CqlUtils and LogDiceUtils/MathUtils are genuine low-level utilities with no project imports; RelationPatternUtils and RelationUtils are domain helpers that happen to live alongside them
issues.suggestion: Rename the package to something that signals its domain dependency, such as 'helpers' or 'support', or move RelationPatternUtils and RelationUtils into the config package (since they operate primarily on RelationConfig/GrammarConfig objects). This removes the misleading implication that 'utils' is a dependency-free bottom layer.

issues.summary: QueryExecutor interface imports config.RelationConfig to provide convenience default methods, coupling the query abstraction to grammar config details
evidence: The default methods executeSurfacePatternForRelation and executeBcqlQueryForRelation call RelationPatternUtils.buildFullPattern — these are pattern-building conveniences that could live in a handler or builder class instead
This import is the query→config half of the circular dependency
issues.suggestion: Remove the default convenience methods from QueryExecutor and move them to a static helper method in the api layer (e.g. SketchHandlers or a new PatternBuilder utility). QueryExecutor would then depend only on model and utils (CqlUtils), cleanly below config in the hierarchy.

root_causes: RelationType was placed in the query package even though it is a grammar configuration concept (what kind of query strategy a relation uses), not a query-execution concept. This misplacement propagated into config importing from query.
The QueryExecutor interface accumulated convenience default methods that reference grammar config objects, crossing the boundary between query execution and configuration.
The utils package was used as a home for domain-specific helpers (operating on RelationConfig/GrammarConfig) rather than only for truly generic utilities, obscuring the real dependency topology.
likely_symptoms: Module-system or OSGi setups would reject the circular config↔query dependency at startup
Developers trying to extract config or query into a separate library would hit a compilation error from the cycle
Testing query components in isolation requires loading config types (and vice versa), inflating unit test setup
possible_false_positives: The utils dependency on config could be intentional if the project treats utils as a 'domain helpers' layer rather than a pure bottom-tier utility layer — the naming is the real issue, not necessarily the coupling

The QueryExecutor default methods are a convenience trade-off explicitly noted in the Javadoc comment ('Intended to keep HTTP-layer handlers free of pattern-construction logic') — a deliberate design choice, though it creates the cycle
